perturbation.original_text: ABW agrees to include the following in the co-branded pages displaying quotes: "All quotes provided by PC Quote, Inc." ABW also agrees to include the following disclaimer on the access page to the co-branded service: "PC Quote is not subject to liability for truth, accuracy, or completeness of the market data information nor is PC Quote liable for errors, mistakes or omissions in the data or for any delays or interruptions in the end user's receipt of the data. PC Quote does not warrant that the data provided may be relied upon for trading purposes."
perturbation.changed_text: ABW should endeavor to include the following in the co-branded pages displaying quotes: "All quotes provided by PC Quote, Inc." ABW also agrees to include the following disclaimer on the access page to the co-branded service: "PC Quote is not subject to liability for truth, accuracy, or completeness of the market data information nor is PC Quote liable for errors, mistakes or omissions in the data or for any delays or interruptions in the end user's receipt of the data. PC Quote does not warrant that the data provided may be relied upon for trading purposes."
perturbation.explanation: Changing "agrees to include" to "should endeavor to include" makes the obligation to include the attribution to PC Quote less strict. This creates ambiguity and makes it easier for ABW to argue they did not violate the contract if the attribution is missing, despite the clear intention for attribution.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Securities Exchange Act of 1934 - Section 10(b) and Rule 10b-5
perturbation.law_explanation: While not directly violating 10b-5, weakening the attribution requirement opens the door for misleading statements. If ABW does not clearly attribute the data source and the data is inaccurate, it can lead to investors making decisions based on faulty information. This could be construed as a deceptive practice, since not correctly attributing the data and using "should endeavor to include" creates an avenue to make misleading statements. This rule prohibits using any device, scheme, or artifice to defraud or engaging in any act, practice, or course of business which operates or would operate as a fraud or deceit upon any person, in connection with the purchase or sale of any security. Removing or weakening the guarantee of attribution of the data source is misleading in that context.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 17 CFR § 240.10b-5 - Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 240.10b-5 - Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. CFR prev | next § 240.10b-5 Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. It shall be unlawful for any person, directly or indirectly, by the use of any means or instrumentality of interstate commerce, or of the mails or of any facility of any national securities exchange , (a) To employ any device, scheme, or artifice to defraud, (b) To make any untrue statement of a material fact or to omit to state a material fact necessary in order to make the statements made, in the light of the circumstances under which they were made, not misleading, or (c) To engage in any act, practice, or course of business which operates or would operate as a fraud or deceit upon any person, in connection with the purchase or sale of any security . (Sec. 10; 48 Stat. 891 ; 15 U.S.C. 78j ) [ 13 FR 8183 , Dec. 22, 1948, as amended at 16 FR 7928 , Aug. 11, 1951] CFR Toolbox Law about... Articles from Wex Table of Popular Names Parallel Table of Authorities
perturbation.justification: The original contract states ABW "agrees to include", whereas the changed contract says ABW "should endeavor to include". The change weakens the obligation for ABW to attribute the data to PC Quote, Inc. The Law Explanation states that the weakened attribution requirement "opens the door for misleading statements" which "could be construed as a deceptive practice". Law Snippet 1, 17 CFR § 240.10b-5, states that it is unlawful to "employ any device, scheme, or artifice to defraud" or "engage in any act, practice, or course of business which operates or would operate as a fraud or deceit upon any person, in connection with the purchase or sale of any security". The modified text creates a situation that could operate as a fraud, thus creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: ABW agrees to sign up all new subscribers to the co-branded service, including execution of all applicable service and exchange agreements, will send to PCQ the executed subscriber agreements before access to the quote servers will be provided to ABW clients by PCQ.
perturbation.changed_text: ABW agrees to attempt to sign up all new subscribers to the co-branded service, including attempting the execution of applicable service and exchange agreements, and will try to send to PCQ the executed subscriber agreements before access to the quote servers will be provided to ABW clients by PCQ.
perturbation.explanation: By changing the mandatory agreement to sign up subscribers and execute agreement to a looser goal of attempting to do so, the text becomes ambiguous. If it is just an attempt, there are no real consequences for failing to sign up subscribers or failing to send the agreements before granting access. This contradicts the intent of the agreement and creates an avenue for ABW to act out of compliance.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 5 prohibits securities brokers/dealers to engage in interstate commerce or to make use of the mails to sell unregistered securities if the security is not exempt or not registered by the SEC. If PCQ's software is used to provide information that is used to facilitate trading in unregistered or non-exempt securities, there is a risk of a violation. This is because PCQ provides the data on which the transactions happen, and if the security in question is unregistered or non-exempt, PCQ (and ABW) are putting themselves in a dangerous position. Therefore, they must be very vigilant about compliance and due diligence. Allowing 'attempts' at signing up subscribers weakens the controls in place that ensures that ABW clients are trading within regulatory bounds, and that PCQ and ABW are not exposed to liability.

Transactions on unregistered exchanges It shall be unlawful for any broker, dealer, or exchange, directly or indirectly, to make use of the mails or any means or instrumentality of interstate commerce for the purpose of using any facility of an exchange within or subject to the jurisdiction of the United States to effect any transaction in a security, or to report any such transaction, unless such exchange (1) is registered as national securities exchange under secti
perturbation.justification: The change from 'agrees to sign up' to 'agrees to attempt to sign up' significantly weakens the obligation. The law explanation highlights the need for vigilance and due diligence to avoid potential liability related to unregistered securities. Scraped Law Snippet 1, 15 USC 78e, makes it unlawful for any broker or dealer to use interstate commerce to effect any transaction in a security, unless the exchange is registered. By changing the language to 'attempt', ABW is not mandated to ensure compliance of new subscribers, which could allow unregistered securities to be traded, thus creating a contradiction and undermining the protections of 15 USC 78e.

perturbation.original_text: The information and data used in the HYPERFEED and SOFTWARE provided under this Agreement, including option prices, stock prices, commodity prices, dividends, dividend dates, volatilities, deltas and other variables, are obtained by PCQ from the various exchanges and other sources which are believed to be reliable and PCQ agrees to run reasonable control checks thereon to verify that the data transmitted by PCQ is the same as the data received from the various exchanges and other sources.
perturbation.changed_text: The information and data used in the HYPERFEED and SOFTWARE provided under this Agreement, including option prices, stock prices, commodity prices, dividends, dividend dates, volatilities, deltas and other variables, are obtained by PCQ from the various exchanges and other sources which may or may not be reliable, and PCQ may or may not run control checks thereon to verify that the data transmitted by PCQ is the same as the data received from the various exchanges and other sources.
perturbation.explanation: Changing "believed to be reliable" and "agrees to run reasonable control checks" to "may or may not be reliable" and "may or may not run control checks" introduces significant ambiguity regarding the reliability of the data and the obligation to verify it. This weakens the assurance of data accuracy. By saying they may not be reliable, and PCQ may or may not run control checks, investors are left to their own devices. This puts PCQ in a more protected legal position.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Securities Exchange Act of 1934 - Section 10(b) and Rule 10b-5
perturbation.law_explanation: This statement is inherently misleading. While the contract includes a disclaimer of liability, explicitly stating that the data sources might not be reliable and that PCQ might not run control checks suggests a reckless disregard for the accuracy of the information. This contradicts Rule 10b-5, which prohibits deceptive practices in connection with the purchase or sale of securities. Providing data known to be potentially unreliable without proper verification could be considered a scheme to defraud investors, violating the law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 17 CFR § 240.10b-5 - Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 240.10b-5 - Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. CFR prev | next § 240.10b-5 Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. It shall be unlawful for any person, directly or indirectly, by the use of any means or instrumentality of interstate commerce, or of the mails or of any facility of any national securities exchange , (a) To employ any device, scheme, or artifice to defraud, (b) To make any untrue statement of a material fact or to omit to state a material fact necessary in order to make the statements made, in the light of the circumstances under which they were made, not misleading, or (c) To engage in any act, practice, or course of business which operates or would operate as a fraud or deceit upon any person, in connection with the purchase or sale of any security . (Sec. 10; 48 Stat. 891 ; 15 U.S.C. 78j ) [ 13 FR 8183 , Dec. 22, 1948, as amended at 16 FR 7928 , Aug. 11, 1951] CFR Toolbox Law about... Articles from Wex Table of Popular Names Parallel Table of Authorities
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity about data reliability and verification, weakening the assurance of data accuracy. The original text states the data is "believed to be reliable" and PCQ "agrees to run reasonable control checks." The changed text states the data "may or may not be reliable" and PCQ "may or may not run control checks." This contradicts Rule 10b-5, which prohibits deceptive practices. Explicitly stating data sources might not be reliable and that PCQ might not run control checks suggests a reckless disregard for accuracy, potentially defrauding investors. Snippet 1 states, "It shall be unlawful for any person, directly or indirectly... (a) To employ any device, scheme, or artifice to defraud, (b) To make any untrue statement of a material fact or to omit to state a material fact necessary in order to make the statements made, in the light of the circumstances under which they were made, not misleading, or (c) To engage in any act, practice, or course of business which operates or would operate as a fraud or deceit upon any person, in connection with the purchase or sale of any security." By weakening the language about data reliability and control checks, the modified text increases the risk of misleading investors, potentially violating Rule 10b-5(b) by omitting a material fact and 10b-5(c) by engaging in a practice that operates as a fraud.
